problem:
The cycle set of a graph G on n vertices is a set A⊆ \{3,\ldots,n\} such that there is a cycle in G of length \ell if and only if \ell ∈ A. Let f(n) count the number of possible such A.

Prove that f(n)=o(2^n).

Prove that f(n)/2^{n/2}→ ∞.
original_latex:
The cycle set of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is a set $A\subseteq \{3,\ldots,n\}$ such that there is a cycle in $G$ of length $\ell$ if and only if $\ell \in A$. Let $f(n)$ count the number of possible such $A$.

Prove that $f(n)=o(2^n)$.

Prove that $f(n)/2^{n/2}\to \infty$.
additional_context:
Conjectured by Erdős and Faudree, who showed that 2^{n/2}<f(n) ≤ 2^{n-2}. The first problem was solved by Verstra\"{e}te \cite{Ve04}, who provedf(n)\ll 2^{n-n^{1/10}}.This was improved by Nenadov \cite{Ne25} tof(n) \ll 2^{n-n^{1/2-o(1)}}.One can also ask about the existence and value of \lim f(n)^{1/n}.

References

[Ne25] R. Nenadov, Improved bound on the number of cycle sets. arXiv:2501.09904 (2025).

[Ve04] Verstra\"{e}te, Jacques, On the number of sets of cycle lengths. Combinatorica (2004), 719-730.
reference_proof_hint:
Let $f(n)$ be the number of sets (A\subseteq{3,\dots,n}) that occur as the **cycle set** of some graph on $n$ vertices.

## 1) Proof that $f(n)=o(2^n)$

A result of Nenadov (improving an earlier bound of Verstraëte) says that there is a function (\varepsilon(n)\to 0) such that
[
f(n)\ \le\ 2^{,n - n^{1/2-\varepsilon(n)}} .
]
([Erdős Problems][1])

Divide by $2^n$:
[
\frac{f(n)}{2^n}\ \le\ 2^{-,n^{1/2-\varepsilon(n)}}.
]
Since (n^{1/2-\varepsilon(n)}\to\infty), the right-hand side tends to $0$. Hence
[
\frac{f(n)}{2^n}\to 0,
]
which is exactly $f(n)=o(2^n)$.

That finishes the first part. ([Erdős Problems][1])

---

## 2) About (\displaystyle \frac{f(n)}{2^{n/2}}\to\infty)

### What we can prove easily: (f(n)\ge 2^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor})

This is Faudree’s lower-bound construction (often stated for even $n$; odd $n$ is similar).

Assume $n$ is even. Label vertices (1,2,\dots,n) and start with the path
[
1-2-3-\cdots-n.
]
Now pick any subset
[
S\subseteq\left{\frac n2+1,\frac n2+2,\dots,n\
